_comment: Chronos Earth — phase-by-phase 2D battle animations. Coordinate space is 0..100 (x, left→right) by 0..70 (y, top→bottom). Each unit's 'pos' array gives its [x,y] in each phase, in order; the app animates between them. 'arrows' belong to a phase and show that phase's key movements. To add a battle: give it an id matching a battle in battles.json and set that battle's hasBattleView:true.

phases.name: Deployment
phases.narration: Harold's Anglo-Saxons form a dense shield wall along the top of Senlac Hill. William's Normans deploy below: archers in front, infantry behind, cavalry in reserve.

phases.name: The Norman assault
phases.narration: Norman archers and infantry struggle uphill against the shield wall and are thrown back. On the left, a rumour spreads that William is dead.

phases.name: The feigned retreats
phases.narration: The Normans fall back — and some Saxons break ranks to chase them downhill. William wheels his cavalry and cuts the pursuers down in the open, repeating the trick.

phases.name: Harold falls
phases.narration: With the shield wall thinned, Norman cavalry and archers break through. Harold is killed — by legend, an arrow to the eye — and the Saxon line collapses.

waterloo.title: Battle of Waterloo
waterloo.subtitle: 18 June 1815 — Napoleon vs the Seventh Coalition

phases.name: Deployment
phases.narration: Wellington's army holds the ridge of Mont-Saint-Jean, anchored on the farms of Hougoumont and La Haye Sainte. Napoleon masses below. Rain has delayed the French start.

phases.name: French assaults
phases.narration: Napoleon attacks Hougoumont as a diversion, then sends d'Erlon's corps against the Allied left-centre. British infantry and a heavy cavalry charge throw them back.

phases.name: Cavalry & crisis
phases.narration: Ney launches massed cavalry charges against the Allied squares without infantry support. La Haye Sainte finally falls — but Wellington's line, though battered, holds. Prussians appear in the east.

phases.name: The Guard recoils
phases.narration: Napoleon throws in his elite Imperial Guard — and for the first time it is repulsed. As the Guard recoils and the Prussians press the flank, the French army dissolves.

phases.name: Deployment
phases.narration: Rome packs a huge, deep infantry mass in the centre to smash straight through. Hannibal forms a thin centre that bulges forward, with veteran African infantry held back on the wings.

phases.name: The Roman push
phases.narration: The Romans drive into Hannibal's centre, which gives ground in a controlled retreat — the bulge inverts into a sagging crescent that draws the Romans in.

phases.name: Cavalry wins the flanks
phases.narration: Hannibal's heavier cavalry routs the Roman horse on both wings, rides around the rear, and returns — sealing the trap.

phases.name: Encirclement
phases.narration: The African wings swing inward and the cavalry closes the rear. The packed Roman army, unable to manoeuvre, is surrounded and destroyed — up to 50,000 killed in a day.

phases.name: Deployment
phases.narration: Darius spreads a vast army across a flattened plain, with scythed chariots in front. Alexander, badly outnumbered, refuses his flanks and edges his whole line to the right.

phases.name: The oblique march
phases.narration: Alexander leads his line rightward, dragging the Persians with him and pulling their formation out of shape. The scythed chariots charge — and are funnelled into gaps and destroyed.

phases.name: The wedge charge
phases.narration: A gap opens in the Persian line. Alexander wheels his Companion cavalry into a wedge and charges straight at Darius himself.

phases.name: Darius flees
phases.narration: With Alexander bearing down and his centre buckling, Darius turns and flees. The Persian army follows — and the Achaemenid Empire is doomed.

phases.name: Deployment
phases.narration: The Greeks deliberately thin their centre and strengthen their wings to match the wider Persian line. The Persians place their best troops in the centre.

phases.name: The charge
phases.narration: To cross the deadly arrow-zone quickly, the Greeks advance at a run — a tactic that astonishes the Persians.

phases.name: Wings win
phases.narration: The strong Greek wings rout the Persian flanks, while the thin Greek centre is pushed back as planned.

phases.name: Double envelopment
phases.narration: Instead of chasing, the victorious wings turn inward and crush the Persian centre. The Persians flee to their ships.

phases.name: Deployment
phases.narration: Henry's small army is hemmed between two woods on a narrow, muddy field. Dismounted men-at-arms hold the centre; longbowmen on the wings plant sharpened stakes. The French knights mass opposite in huge numbers.

phases.name: Henry advances
phases.narration: Outnumbered and waiting, Henry advances to longbow range and unleashes an arrow storm, goading the French into attacking across the mud.

phases.name: Into the mud
phases.narration: The heavily armoured French knights struggle forward through deep mud, funnelled together by the woods and torn apart by arrows from the flanks.

phases.name: French rout
phases.narration: Exhausted and crushed together, the French are cut down or captured. A few thousand English have shattered the flower of French chivalry.

phases.name: The bait
phases.narration: Napoleon deliberately leaves his right flank weak and cedes the commanding Pratzen Heights, tempting the Allies to attack there and march down off the high ground.

phases.name: The Allies take the bait
phases.narration: The Allies pour their main strength south to crush the French right, stripping their own centre off the Pratzen Heights.

phases.name: Soult storms the centre
phases.narration: Napoleon launches Soult's corps in a sudden assault up the now-undefended Pratzen, splitting the Allied army in two.

phases.name: Collapse
phases.narration: With the heights taken, the French roll up both halves of the Allied army. The southern wing is driven onto frozen ponds and shattered.

phases.name: The lines form
phases.narration: The Franco-Spanish fleet sails in a long single line. Nelson forms his fleet into two columns aimed perpendicular at that line — a bold plan to cut it into pieces.

phases.name: The columns close in
phases.narration: Under fire, the two British columns drive straight at the enemy line, aiming to break through at two points.

phases.name: Breaking the line
phases.narration: The columns punch through, raking the enemy ships bow-to-stern at close range and starting a series of brutal individual duels.

phases.name: Decisive victory
phases.narration: The cut-apart enemy van cannot help the rear. The British capture or destroy much of the fleet. Nelson is mortally wounded, but Britain rules the seas.

gettysburg.title: Battle of Gettysburg
gettysburg.subtitle: 1–3 July 1863 — turning point of the Civil War

phases.name: Day 1 — the armies collide
phases.narration: The armies meet by chance at Gettysburg. Confederates push the outnumbered Union forces back through the town to the high ground south of it.

phases.name: Day 2 — the flanks
phases.narration: The Union holds a strong 'fishhook' line along the ridges. Confederate attacks hammer both flanks — the Wheatfield, and the desperate defence of Little Round Top.

phases.name: Day 3 — Pickett's Charge
phases.narration: Lee gambles on a frontal assault: 12,500 men march across open ground toward the Union centre on Cemetery Ridge.

phases.name: Repulse
phases.narration: Artillery and rifle fire tear the charge apart at the 'High Water Mark'. The shattered Confederates fall back, and Lee retreats south — the Confederacy never recovers.

d-day.title: D-Day — The Normandy Landings
d-day.subtitle: 6 June 1944 — the Western Front is opened

phases.name: Airborne & bombardment
phases.narration: Overnight, paratroopers drop inland to seize bridges and causeways. At dawn, a vast fleet bombards the German 'Atlantic Wall' defences along five code-named beaches.

phases.name: The landings
phases.narration: Landing craft hit five beaches — Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno and Sword. At Omaha the troops are pinned down under murderous fire and take heavy casualties.

phases.name: Off the beaches
phases.narration: By nightfall the Allies have clawed their way off every beach and begin linking the beachheads together against fierce German resistance.

phases.name: The lodgement holds
phases.narration: Despite the bloody cost, the foothold holds. Over the following days a million men and their supplies pour ashore — the liberation of Western Europe has begun.

thermopylae.title: Battle of Thermopylae
thermopylae.subtitle: August 480 BCE — 7,000 Greeks against the Persian invasion

phases.name: The Hot Gates
phases.narration: Between the mountains and the sea runs a pass barely wide enough for a cart. Leonidas posts his 300 Spartans and some 7,000 allies across it, behind an old wall. Xerxes waits four days for them to flee. They do not.

phases.name: Waves break on the wall
phases.narration: For two days Persian assaults crash into the Greek shield wall — even the King’s elite Immortals are thrown back. In the narrows, numbers mean nothing; the long Greek spears mean everything.

phases.name: The mountain path
phases.narration: A local shepherd, Ephialtes, betrays the secret: a goat track over the mountain. By night the Immortals climb it, brush aside the Phocian guard, and descend behind the Greek line.

phases.name: The last stand
phases.narration: Leonidas dismisses most of the army. The Spartans, Thespians and Thebans who remain fight on a low hillock — spears, then swords, then hands and teeth — until arrows finish it. The pass falls, and becomes a legend.

stalingrad.title: Battle of Stalingrad
stalingrad.subtitle: Aug 1942 – Feb 1943 — the turning point of the Eastern Front

phases.name: To the Volga
phases.narration: Paulus’s 6th Army storms the city on the great river that bears Stalin’s name. The Luftwaffe burns it to rubble — and the rubble becomes a fortress. Chuikov’s 62nd Army holds a strip of bank sometimes only hundreds of metres deep.

phases.name: Rattenkrieg
phases.narration: “The war of the rats”: grain silo, tractor works, Pavlov’s house — every cellar and stairwell a front line. The Germans take ninety percent of the city and cannot take the rest.

phases.name: Operation Uranus
phases.narration: In November, Zhukov springs the trap: Soviet tank armies smash through the weak Romanian flanks far to the north and south, and meet at Kalach behind the city. A quarter of a million men are sealed in the pocket.

phases.name: The pocket dies
phases.narration: Göring’s promised air bridge fails; relief attempts freeze short. Starving in the snow, promoted to Field Marshal so he would not surrender — Paulus surrenders. Germany never fully recovers.

tours.title: Battle of Tours
tours.subtitle: 732 CE — Charles Martel halts the Umayyad advance

phases.name: Deployment
phases.narration: Charles Martel forms his seasoned Frankish infantry into a dense square on wooded high ground and simply waits. Abd al-Rahman masses his cavalry-heavy Umayyad army below. For six days the two armies watch each other.

phases.name: The cavalry charges
phases.narration: The Umayyad horsemen charge the Frankish square again and again. The Franks stand shoulder to shoulder — a chronicler wrote they held "like a wall of ice" — and every charge breaks against the shield line.

phases.name: A raid on the camp
phases.narration: Frankish scouts slip round to threaten the Umayyad camp and its plunder. A rumour races through the ranks that the loot is being stolen — and part of the Umayyad cavalry peels away to save it, tearing a gap in the line.

phases.name: Abd al-Rahman falls
phases.narration: Trying to steady his wavering horsemen, Abd al-Rahman is surrounded and cut down. Leaderless, the Umayyads slip away in the night. The Frankish square never broke — and Charles earns the name "Martel", the Hammer.

midway.title: Battle of Midway
midway.subtitle: 4–7 June 1942 — four carriers lost, the Pacific War turns

phases.name: The ambush is set
phases.narration: Warned by codebreakers, Admiral Spruance waits northeast of Midway with three carriers. Nagumo’s four-carrier Kidō Butai steams in from the west, unaware, and turns to soften up the atoll.

phases.name: Strike on Midway
phases.narration: Japanese planes bomb Midway but fail to knock it out. Nagumo orders his reserve aircraft re-armed for a second land strike — just as a scout reports American carriers to the northeast. Bombs and torpedoes lie stacked on his decks.

phases.name: The torpedo squadrons
phases.narration: Caught mid-rearming, Nagumo hesitates. US torpedo bombers attack first, low and alone, and are almost annihilated — but they drag the defending Zeros down to the waves and leave the sky above the carriers empty.

phases.name: Five minutes that changed the war
phases.narration: US dive bombers plunge in unopposed and set Akagi, Kaga and Sōryū ablaze in minutes. Hiryū’s planes cripple Yorktown before she too is lost. Japan loses four fleet carriers — and the initiative in the Pacific.

phases.name: Deployment
phases.narration: Hannibal masses eighty war elephants across his front, three lines of infantry behind — mercenaries, then Carthaginian citizens, then his hardened veterans of Italy held in reserve. Scipio lines up his maniples with deliberate lanes running straight back through them, and puts his cavalry on the wings: Masinissa’s Numidians on the right, Laelius’s Romans on the left.

phases.name: The elephant charge fails
phases.narration: The elephants charge. Roman horns and trumpets blare, panicking many; the rest are funnelled harmlessly down Scipio’s open lanes and out the rear. On both wings the stronger Roman and Numidian cavalry rout Hannibal’s horsemen and gallop off in pursuit.

phases.name: The lines collide
phases.narration: The infantry meet head-on. Scipio’s hastati and principes grind through Hannibal’s first two lines, but his fresh veterans stand firm. Scipio breaks off, halts the pursuit, and reforms his whole army into one long line to face them.

phases.name: The cavalry returns
phases.narration: At the decisive moment Masinissa and Laelius return from the chase and smash into the rear of Hannibal’s veterans. Trapped front and back, the last Carthaginian line is destroyed. Hannibal escapes; the Second Punic War is over, and Rome has won the western Mediterranean.

annexation-of-crimea.title: Annexation of Crimea
annexation-of-crimea.subtitle: Feb–Mar 2014 — a seizure without a pitched battle

phases.name: The little green men
phases.narration: Soldiers in unmarked green uniforms appear overnight at the Crimean parliament, airports and crossroads. Not a shot is fired — no one will say whose they are.

phases.name: Bases blockaded
phases.narration: Ukrainian garrisons wake to find themselves surrounded and sealed in. Weeks of tense standoffs at the gates — sieges of patience, not battles.

phases.name: The referendum
phases.narration: A vote on joining Russia is staged under armed occupation. Most of the world calls it illegitimate; Moscow calls it the will of the people.

phases.name: Annexation declared
phases.narration: Russia formally absorbs the peninsula. Ukrainian forces withdraw to the mainland. The annexation is recognised by almost no one — and the war it opened has never ended.

phases.name: The trap is baited
phases.narration: Themistocles sends Xerxes a secret message: the Greeks mean to flee. The Persian fleet rows into the narrow strait by night to seal it — exactly where numbers count for nothing.

phases.name: Into the narrows
phases.narration: At dawn the Phoenician vanguard pushes up the strait. Hull crowds against hull; the great fleet can no longer manoeuvre.

phases.name: The Greek line strikes
phases.narration: The Greeks back water — then ram. Bronze beaks stave in the jammed Persian hulls; admiral Ariabignes falls; Artemisia escapes by sinking a ship of her own side.

phases.name: Rout under the king's eyes
phases.narration: The Persians break south, fouling their own oncoming rear squadrons. Xerxes watches it all from a throne on Mount Aigaleo — and soon turns for home.

fall-of-constantinople.title: Fall of Constantinople
fall-of-constantinople.subtitle: 6 April – 29 May 1453 — the last day of Byzantium

phases.name: The siege lines close
phases.narration: Mehmed's host entrenches before the Theodosian walls and the great bombard begins its work — stone balls of half a tonne against walls that had stood a thousand years.

phases.name: Ships over the hills
phases.narration: Denied the Golden Horn by the great chain, Mehmed drags seventy ships overland on greased timbers behind Galata — and launches them inside the harbour. The defenders must now man every wall at once.

phases.name: Assaults repelled
phases.narration: Wave after wave breaks on the stockades patching the Lycus valley breach. Giustiniani's Genoese hold; mines are counter-mined; the city endures.

phases.name: The final assault
phases.narration: Before dawn on 29 May: irregulars first, then Anatolians, then the Janissaries. Giustiniani falls wounded and is carried off; a postern gate, the Kerkoporta, is found unbarred.

phases.name: The city falls
phases.narration: Constantine XI dies fighting at the walls, his body never surely found. Eleven centuries of Byzantium end; Mehmed rides to Hagia Sophia and turns it to a mosque.

red-cliffs.title: Battle of Red Cliffs
red-cliffs.subtitle: Winter 208–209 CE — fire on the Yangtze

phases.name: Stalemate on the river
phases.narration: Cao Cao's northern host, wracked by sickness and unused to water, chains its ships together for steadiness on the winter Yangtze — a floating fortress, and a single target.

phases.name: The feigned defection
phases.narration: Veteran general Huang Gai sends word he will defect. His squadron stands out for the northern fleet — decks stacked with kindling, oil and dry reeds under the surrender flags.

phases.name: Fire on the wind
phases.narration: The southeast wind rises. Lit and abandoned at the last moment, the fire ships drive into the chained fleet — flame leaps hull to hull and into the shore camps.

phases.name: The Huarong road
phases.narration: Cao Cao burns what the fire spared and flees overland through the marshes, his army dissolving in the mud. The south is lost to him — the Three Kingdoms begin here.

battle-of-britain.title: Battle of Britain
battle-of-britain.subtitle: Jul–Oct 1940 — the first battle fought entirely in the air

phases.name: Kanalkampf
phases.narration: July: probing attacks on Channel convoys and the ports. Dowding husbands his squadrons — the radar chain watches everything that crosses the water.

phases.name: The hardest days
phases.narration: August: the airfields and radar stations are hammered day after day. Fighter Command bends near breaking — pilots fly four sorties a day and sleep where they land.

phases.name: The switch to London
phases.narration: 7 September: the Luftwaffe turns on the capital. Terror for Londoners — and an unintended reprieve for the broken airfields, which Park uses to rebuild.

phases.name: Battle of Britain Day
phases.narration: 15 September: two massed raids are met and broken by every squadron 11 and 12 Group can field. Two days later Hitler postpones the invasion indefinitely. 'Never was so much owed by so many to so few.'

pharsalus.title: Battle of Pharsalus
pharsalus.subtitle: 48 BCE — Caesar breaks Pompey in Rome’s civil war

phases.name: Deployment
phases.narration: Both armies form the traditional three lines, each anchoring a flank on the River Enipeus to the west. Outnumbered, Caesar hides a fourth line of cohorts behind his right, where Pompey has massed nearly all his cavalry under Labienus to sweep round and envelop him.

phases.name: The lines meet
phases.narration: Caesar’s infantry advance, pause to regroup, then charge home. In the centre the two masses of legionaries lock together and Pompey’s line holds.

phases.name: Labienus’s cavalry charge
phases.narration: Pompey’s massed cavalry charges Caesar’s outnumbered horsemen on the eastern flank and drives them back, wheeling to fall on Caesar’s rear.

phases.name: The fourth line strikes
phases.narration: Caesar springs his hidden fourth line, whose men thrust their pila up at the riders’ faces. The cavalry panics and flees to the hills, baring Pompey’s left. Caesar’s veteran third line rolls it up; Pompey loses heart, quits the field, and flees to Egypt.

crecy.title: Battle of Crécy
crecy.subtitle: 26 August 1346 — the longbow shatters French chivalry

phases.name: Deployment
phases.narration: Edward III dismounts his men-at-arms in three divisions on a gentle slope between Crécy and Wadicourt, longbowmen thrown forward on the wings behind dug pits. The Black Prince holds the forward division. The French host arrives late in the day, strung out and disordered.

phases.name: The Genoese are broken
phases.narration: Philip sends his Genoese crossbowmen forward first, but without their pavises and slowed by mud they are outranged and outshot. Cut down in minutes, they break and flee back into the advancing French line.

phases.name: The cavalry charges
phases.narration: Enraged, the French knights charge uphill through their own fleeing crossbowmen, into mud and an arrow storm — as many as fifteen or sixteen charges through the evening. Each loses its momentum before it reaches the line; the Black Prince’s division is hard-pressed but holds.

phases.name: The French melt away
phases.narration: Every charge shatters against the stakes, pits and arrows. The blind King John of Bohemia is killed after tying his bridle to his knights’; Alençon and Lorraine fall too. Philip, an arrow in his jaw and two horses killed under him, flees into the night.

kursk.title: Battle of Kursk
kursk.subtitle: July–August 1943 — the German offensive that broke on Soviet defences

phases.name: The salient & Operation Citadel
phases.narration: The front bulges deep to the west around Kursk. Germany plans a double envelopment — Model’s 9th Army striking south from Orel, Hoth’s 4th Panzer Army and Kempf striking north from Belgorod — to pinch off the salient. The Soviets have dug six defensive belts nearly 300 km deep and laid close to a million mines.

phases.name: The northern pincer stalls
phases.narration: Model attacks south on 5 July but grinds into dense minefields and anti-tank fronts. After bloody fighting around Ponyri and Olkhovatka the northern pincer makes only limited gains and halts.

phases.name: The southern pincer drives deep
phases.narration: Hoth’s panzers, more heavily armoured, push far deeper through the Voronezh Front’s defences, grinding north-east toward the rail town of Prokhorovka with Kempf guarding the eastern flank.

phases.name: Prokhorovka
phases.narration: On 12 July hundreds of tanks collide in the open fields at Prokhorovka as the Soviet 5th Guards Tank Army throws itself head-on at the panzer spearhead. Both sides bleed heavily and the German advance is fought to a standstill.

phases.name: The Soviet counteroffensives
phases.narration: With Citadel stalled, the Soviets unleash Operation Kutuzov against Orel and Operation Rumyantsev toward Belgorod and Kharkov. The Germans are levered back off the salient — the first time a great German summer offensive is halted, and the initiative in the east passes to the USSR for good.

pearl-harbor.title: Attack on Pearl Harbor
pearl-harbor.subtitle: 7 December 1941 — the surprise strike that brought America into the war

phases.name: Approach at dawn
phases.narration: Nagumo’s six-carrier strike force lies undetected north of Oahu and launches its aircraft before sunrise. In the harbour the Pacific Fleet lies at anchor, ammunition locked and planes parked wingtip to wingtip — and, crucially, its own aircraft carriers are away at sea.

phases.name: First wave
phases.narration: At 07:48 the first wave of some 183 aircraft falls on an unwarned harbour. Shallow-running torpedoes and armour-piercing bombs tear into Battleship Row: the Arizona blows up and the Oklahoma rolls over, while dive-bombers wreck the airfields.

phases.name: Second wave
phases.narration: Around 08:50 a second wave of some 170 aircraft renews the assault on ships and airfields — but now the defenders are awake, and a storm of anti-aircraft fire meets it and downs more of the attackers.

phases.name: The strike force turns away
phases.narration: Eight battleships are sunk or crippled and over 2,400 Americans are dead. But the absent carriers survive, and Nagumo declines a third strike against the fuel tanks and repair yards — leaving intact the base from which the Pacific war would be won.
